summary: I was born in Boston, Massachusetts while my father was in graduate school in the United States. Even though I was born there, I didn’t grow up in Boston because my family moved back to Venezuela. Being an American citizen from Boston has always been part of my identity, especially when I decided to leave Venezuela for safety reasons.

I was raised in Venezuela, but I left in my mid-twenties due to political instability, economic difficulties, and concerns for my personal safety. Life there was dangerous; I remember being at a party with 17 people and 12 handguns on the table, including mine. I knew I had to leave to find a better future, which led me to the United States.

Caracas is where I come from, a city with about 6.5 to 7 million people. Growing up there shaped much of my early life, but the instability pushed me to seek opportunities elsewhere. Leaving Caracas was difficult, but necessary for my safety and future.

After leaving Venezuela, I moved to Chicago and lived in the Loop area, which is very big and metropolitan. I expanded my job search there but eventually decided to look towards Minneapolis for better opportunities. Chicago was an important step in my transition to life in the U.S.

The Loop in Chicago was where I lived when I first arrived in the city. It’s a very large and metropolitan area, and living there gave me a sense of the urban American experience. However, I eventually moved on to Minneapolis to pursue job opportunities.

I moved to Minneapolis in my late twenties around 2003 after expanding my job search from Chicago. I got a job at a marketing agency here and originally planned to stay for just a year. But over time, I built a life here with my wife and two kids, and now I consider Minneapolis my adopted home.

Minnesota has been my home since 2003, and I’ve spent over a quarter of my life here. It’s a great place to live—safe, educated, and perfect for raising a family. I feel fortunate to have settled here and have dedicated myself to giving back to the community through my work.

I founded Raices Media to help the Latino community by providing resources and support. The company has three main areas: publications, multicultural outreach through Diversity into Action, and marketing programs via Hispanic Connect. Raices Media reflects my commitment to addressing disparities and helping minorities succeed in Minnesota.

Hispanic Tiempo Magazine is one of the two publications under Raices Media that I oversee. It provides valuable resources to the Hispanic community, helping them navigate education, healthcare, and other systems. This magazine is part of my effort to give back and support immigrants like myself.

Vida y Deportes, which means Life and Sports, is the second magazine I publish through Raices Media. It focuses on providing community resources and promoting a healthy, active lifestyle among Hispanics. This publication complements Hispanic Tiempo Magazine in serving the community’s needs.

Diversity into Action is the cornerstone of my business and focuses on multicultural outreach. We organize career fairs, job expos, and resource events to help minorities find employment and start businesses. This organization embodies my passion for helping others overcome barriers and succeed in the U.S.

Hispanic Connect is another multicultural outreach organization I run through Raices Media. We create marketing programs that connect companies with minority segments, helping businesses reach diverse audiences effectively. It’s part of my broader mission to support minority communities in Minnesota.
